days.headline: Into the Alps at last, capped by a savage, little-used summit finish at the Plateau de Solaison — with a rest day to follow, expect the GC favourites to empty the tank.

picks.why: The dominant climber of the race so far and the rider most able to press an advantage on a summit this steep.
The rider with the best track record of matching Pogačar on sustained high-mountain climbs, with a rest day cushion afterward to recover.
Has stayed glued to the front of the GC group in the mountains all race and is a threat to move on the toughest summit finish yet.

days.headline: The race against the clock along the shore of Lake Geneva from Évian to Thonon — flat, fast, pure power-TT terrain.

picks.why: A former world time-trial champion and the strongest pure specialist against the watch in the field.
Loses very little to the dedicated TT men and could extend his GC lead further on a course this fast.
A recognised TT specialist who can post a top result even outside the GC picture.

days.headline: A lumpy transition through the foothills of the Chartreuse to Voiron — nominally a sprint stage, but textured enough that a well-drilled breakaway could steal it before the high Alps arrive in earnest.

picks.why: Versatile enough to win from a reduced group if the terrain shells the pure sprinters before the line.
Has been a fixture on the podium in reduced bunch finishes all race.
A craft breakaway rider who specialises in exactly this kind of lumpy, last-chance-before-the-mountains stage.

days.headline: A long Alpine haul to the historic ski station at Orcières-Merlette — a steady rather than savage finale that suits a sustained, team-led GC assault.

picks.why: The strongest team and the strongest rider on any climb where a long, sustained effort rather than a single sharp kick decides things.
Built for exactly this kind of steady, high-altitude grind rather than a punchy uphill sprint.
Has climbed consistently with the GC group all race and a long, even gradient plays to his engine.

days.headline: Short, sharp and explosive: under 130 km from Gap to the 21 hairpins of Alpe d'Huez — a stage built for chaos and long-range attacks rather than a controlled train.

picks.why: The rider best equipped to go long-range on a short mountain stage where explosive, decisive racing suits him most.
Historically dangerous when a short, sharp mountain stage invites early, race-breaking moves rather than a slow burn.
An aggressive climber who thrives in the kind of unpredictable, attack-heavy racing a short summit finish like this breeds.

fantasy.strategy: The stage 15-21 transfer window opens today, so use fresh transfers now to load up on GC climbers ahead of the brutal Plateau de Solaison summit finish — this run of stages is climbing-heavy (15, 18, 19 and 20 are all mountains), so a pure sprinter is dead weight for most of the window. The Évian-Thonon time trial is the one clear window to also field a TT specialist without sacrificing climbing points elsewhere. Save the double-points bonus stage for one of the marquee mountain days later in the window — Alpe d'Huez back-to-back on stages 19 and 20 is the standout candidate — and keep enough budget in reserve to react once the road to Paris starts to take shape.
